content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 17, 2017

Heather McLaughlin
City Attorney
City of Benicia
City Hall 250 East L Street
Benicia, CA 94510

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-17-257

Dear Ms. McLaughlin:

       This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Patterson,
Councilmember Campbell, and Councilmember Schwartzman regarding the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice
under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest
prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

       Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

       Do Mayor Patterson, Councilmember Campbell or Councilmember Schwartzman have a
conflict of interest in decisions regarding the creation of zoning standards regulating cannabis
businesses ("Cannabis Ordinance")?

CONCLUSION

       Councilmember Schwartzman has a conflict of interest in the Cannabis Ordinance decision
in that he owns commercial property in the area subject to rezoning under the Ordinance. Due to the
commercial property's location, the public generally exception does not apply. The other two
officials do not have a conflict of interest.

_______________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-257
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Benicia City Council is a five-member body which includes an elected mayor. The
City's current regulations prohibit cannabis businesses. On December 5, 2017, the City Council will
consider adopting an ordinance establishing zoning standards regulating cannabis businesses
("Cannabis Ordinance"). As part of the Cannabis Ordinance, the Council will consider establishing
"buffers." The Cannabis Ordinance, as drafted, would not allow cannabis businesses within 500 feet
of a city park, or within 600 feet of a school. There are over 11,717 residential units in the City with
a concentration of residences in the downtown area. The draft Cannabis Ordinance would allow
cannabis businesses in much of the City's Downtown Mixed Use Master Plan (DMUMP) area, but
certain areas are impacted by the proposed buffers. The Planning Commission has recommended
that cannabis businesses not be allowed in the DMUMP area.

Mayor Patterson owns and resides in a single-family residence, located in a residential zone,
within 500 feet of a shopping center in the DMUMP area. You state her residential property is
"similar to at least 25% of other residential property in the jurisdiction" and, that "[b]ecause of the
concentration of residences in the downtown area, it is apparent on the map that her situation is
pretty much like other residential properties." The residence cannot be used for a dispensary or
other cannabis use. Additionally, under the proposed buffers, the shopping center could not be
used for a dispensary or other cannabis use.

Councilmember Campbell owns a single-family residence within 500 feet of a commercial
area known as "Solano Square." This area contains a number of different commercial
developments. You state that his residential property is "similar to the Mayor's in terms of
similarity to the rest of the residential properties and uniqueness." He also owns commercial real
property in the DMUMP area where he runs his dental office. His parcel is zoned Neighborhood
General-Open and is surrounded by the Neighborhood General zone. Councilmember Campbell's
business is within 500 feet of a park. Under the proposed buffers, Councilmember Campbell's
commercial property could not be used for a dispensary or other cannabis use.

Councilmember Schwartzman owns the property where he lives and from which he runs his
mortgage business. The property is in the DMUMP area, and his parcel is zoned Town
Core. Under the Cannabis Ordinance, Councilmember Schwartzman's property would be a
potential site for a cannabis business, regardless of the proposed buffers. You note that if the
Council enacts the ordinance as proposed, his commercial property "would be one of
approximately 6 blocks of property that can be used for a dispensary on First Street." According to
your understanding, the sales price of at least one commercial property on First Street increased
substantially once the potential for locating a dispensary on First Street became public.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public

File No. A-17-257
Page No. 3

generally, on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. As applicable to the facts provide, Section 87103 identifies the following interests:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

For the decision at issue, Mayor Patterson has a real property interest in her single-family residence; Councilmember Campbell has a real property interest in his residence and a business entity, real property, and a source of income interest in his commercial real property; and Councilmember Schwartzman has a business entity, real property and source of income interest in his commercial real property.²

Foreseeability and Materiality

A decision's financial effect on an official's interest is presumed reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest is the "subject" of a proceeding if the decision involves any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest, including a decision affecting real property under Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) provides that a financial interest is material if the decision:

(2) Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning (other than a zoning decision applicable to all properties designated in that category), annexation or de-annexation, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district, or other local government subdivision, or other boundaries. . .

Councilmember Schwartzman: Based on the facts presented, Councilmember Schwartzman's real property in the DMUMP area is subject to rezoning to allow cannabis businesses under the Cannabis Ordinance. Therefore, the decision is deemed to have a material, reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Councilmember Schwartzman's real property interest under Regulation 18702.2(a)(2). He must recuse himself from the decision.

Where the interest is not a named party in or the subject of the decision, the decision's financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more

² While Councilmember Campbell and Schwartzman have financial interests in their respective businesses, these businesses are not implicated in the Cannabis Ordinance decisions and we do not further analyze these interests.

File No. A-17-257
Page No. 4

than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) Different materiality standards apply
depending on the nature of interest. Standards for real property are found in Regulation 18702.2.

Mayor Patterson: As applicable to her real property interest in her single-family residence,
Regulation 18702.2 (a)(11) provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on an
official's real property interest is material if the decision affects real property value for property
located within 500 feet of the property line of the official's residential property. Nonetheless, "the
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will
be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property."

Under the facts presented, Mayor Patterson's real property interest is located within 500 feet
of the shopping center within the DMUMP. However, the facts indicate that her residential property
is one of many in the concentrated area of residences in the downtown area. Even if the shopping
center didn't fall within the proposed buffer, there is no indication that permitting a cannabis
commercial business to conduct business in the shopping center would impact her residential
property in a different manner than the existing center's businesses such as increased traffic or
intensity of use. Therefore, Mayor Patterson does not have a conflict of interest and may participate
in the Ordinance decisions.

Councilmember Campbell: Like Mayor Patterson's interest in a single-family residence, it
does not appear that allowing a cannabis business to operate within 500 feet of the property would
affect his property value or impact the traffic or use intensity for his residential real property. He
does not have a conflict of interest based on his residence.

For his commercial property containing his business entities, we apply the materiality
standard for business entities in Regulation 18702.1. (See Regulation 18702.2 (a)(11).) Applicable
to these facts, Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that the financial effect is material if a prudent
person with sufficient information would find that the decision would contribute to a change in the
business' value. Examples include decisions that would:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity
in which the business entity is engaged;

(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the
business entity is engaged;

(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business
entity supplies;

(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment
projects, traffic road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either
temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives;

(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other
project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws
from the location of the project; or

File No. A-17-257
Page No. 5

(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the
business entity.

Councilmember Campbell owns a commercial property in the DMUMP area. However,
Councilmember Campbell's property is not itself available for use by a cannabis business either
under the current laws or the proposed Ordinance. Therefore, none of the above factors are
implicated here. Councilmember Campbell does not have a conflict of interest and may participate
in the Ordinance decisions.

Public Generally Exception

Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the
decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the general rule:

A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest
is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his
or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant
segment.

To invoke the exception, the official must establish that a significant segment of the public
is affected and the effect on the official's interest is not unique compared to the effect on the
significant segment. A "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25 percent of (1) the
businesses or nonprofit entities; (2) the real property, commercial real property, or residential real
property; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).)

Here, Councilmember Schwartzman's commercial property is unique in its location on First
Street as a potential location for a cannabis business. If the park buffers are enacted, his property
would be one of approximately six blocks of property that can be used for a dispensary on First
Street. You have also noted that commercial property with the potential for use as a dispensary on
First Street has already seen an impact in property value. It appears that the financial effect of the
Ordinance will uniquely affect Councilmember Schwartzman's First Street commercial property
and the public generally exception does not apply.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

businesses ("Cannabis Ordinance")?

officials do not have a conflict of interest.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do Mayor Patterson, Councilmember Campbell or Councilmember Schwartzman have a 
conflict of interest in decisions regarding the creation of zoning standards regulating cannabis 
businesses ("Cannabis Ordinance")?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Schwartzman has a conflict of interest in the Cannabis Ordinance decision 
in that he owns commercial property in the area subject to rezoning under the Ordinance. Due to the 
commercial property's location, the public generally exception does not apply. The other two 
officials do not have a conflict of interest.
